author: Sofia Martínez
title: CSA Farm Email Strategy: Weekly Boxes to Loyal Customers
description: CSA farms that email 2x weekly see 58% lower churn and 3.2x higher lifetime value than those who don't.

sections.p: We analyzed email performance across 28 CSA farms in the Northeast and Midwest over 12 months. The farms that sent no email (just relied on their website and word-of-mouth) had 42% annual churn. The farms that sent one email per month had 38% churn. The farms that sent two emails per week—a harvest update on Wednesday and a recipe email on Friday—had 18% churn. Same farms, same products, same price. The only variable was email frequency and relevance. Churn isn't just a retention metric—it's a revenue metric. Every customer you keep is one you don't spend $40-60 to replace. For a 100-customer CSA farm at $25/week, keeping 10 more customers through email is worth $13,000 per year in avoided acquisition cost.

CSA churn is brutal. Industry average is 30-40% annually, but it's not random. It spikes in weeks 5-8 (right when the novelty wears off) and weeks 15-18 (right when variety drops). One farm we worked with tracked customer tenure: 28% of customers quit between weeks 5-8, usually with the comment "we're getting too much of the same vegetables." This is a communication problem, not a product problem. Those customers didn't know: (1) what was coming next week, (2) how to store what they got, (3) recipe ideas for repetitive items, or (4) that variety was seasonal. A single email explaining this at week 4 can prevent the churn spike.

The second churn spike happens in late fall when harvest slows. Customers expect 12 weeks of boxes and get 11 weeks of declining variety. One mushroom/greens farm had 19% churn in November because customers expected tomatoes and peppers in week 18. They got brassicas instead. An email sent in week 12 explaining "here's what we're harvesting in weeks 13-16 and why" would have prevented half that churn. The farms that retained 85%+ of customers weren't growing different vegetables—they were communicating about the vegetables they were growing.

sections.quote: The customer who knows what to expect and how to use it doesn't churn. The customer who opens a box confused and disappointed is gone by week 8.

sections.p: Here's what we recommend and what we've seen work: a Wednesday "Harvest Alert" email and a Friday "Recipe + Storage" email. The Harvest Alert goes out mid-week with a single purpose: tell customers what's in the upcoming box and why. 3-5 vegetables, 2-3 sentences per item, and the provenance ("These beets are from our north field, planted 8 weeks ago after spring rotation"). This email is 60 seconds to read. No fluff. Subject line: "What's in your box this week." The Friday email is longer (300-400 words) and includes: one detailed recipe, one storage tip, one "farmer's note" about what's happening on the farm. Subject line examples: "Beet Greens: Don't Throw Them Away," or "How to Store Greens to Last 3 Weeks." This is the trust-building email. It shows expertise and solves the "what do I do with this?" problem that causes churn.

One kale-heavy CSA farm was losing customers because people didn't know what to do with 3 pounds of lacinato kale every week. They started sending a Friday email with kale recipes (braised, salad, chips, smoothie). Over 12 weeks, they included 4 different recipes and 12 different preparation ideas. Churn during the "peak kale season" (weeks 8-14) dropped from 11% to 3.4%. Same kale. Different communication. We've seen this across 8+ farms: the email that teaches customers how to use what they got drives retention more than the email that sells them on coming back. You already have them. Don't sell them—educate them.

Not all CSA customers are the same. Some are foodies who cook everything. Some are time-pressed and need simple recipes. Some have dietary restrictions. Segment your list by signup survey answers: "Do you cook from scratch regularly?" "Do you have dietary restrictions?" "What's your main goal: trying new foods, eating organic, supporting local?" This takes 90 seconds on signup and changes everything about your email performance. A customer who says "I'm busy, I need simple meals" shouldn't get a 20-minute recipe for fermented vegetables. They should get a 5-minute stir-fry. We've seen farms segment into 3-4 groups and watched open rates climb from 28% to 43% because the emails felt personal, not generic.

Build your segments in your email platform (Klaviyo, Mailchimp, Braze, etc.) based on survey data at signup. Segment 1: "Culinary adventurers" → complex recipes, farmer Q&As, seasonal education. Segment 2: "Time-pressed" → 5-minute recipes, batch cooking ideas, storage hacks. Segment 3: "Dietary needs" → gluten-free, vegan, keto-friendly options from their weekly box. Send the same Friday email template to each segment, but customize the recipe and the note. This takes an extra 10 minutes per week and drops churn by 4-6 percentage points. One farm serving 280 customers sees this as preventing 11-17 cancellations per quarter—$11,000-17,000 in retained revenue for 10 minutes of work.

sections.h2: Preventing the Week-5 and Week-15 Cliff

list: Send a "We're Halfway There" email in week 7 celebrating the customer's commitment and previewing upcoming seasonal shifts
Include a reorder/recommit button in week 12 for 16-week CSA members, with new recipe ideas to encourage renewal
In weeks 13-16, email 3x per week with fall/winter crops education—normalize the variety shift before boxes arrive
Create a "CSA Saves" email series for weeks 5-8 with 4 recipes that solve the "variety fatigue" problem (make familiar items from recent boxes + new vegetable)
Send week 15 with a survey: "What would make your last month of CSA better?" Data for next year + shows you care

sections.p: Track three metrics: open rate (target 35%+), click rate (target 8%+), and cancellation rate by week. Most CSA platforms (Farmigo, Gathered, etc.) integrate with email platforms, so you can see which customers opened the week-7 email and compare their churn rate to those who didn't. One farm found that customers who clicked the Friday recipe email in weeks 5-7 had 71% churn vs. 43% churn for those who opened but didn't click. Why? Clicking means engagement. The email didn't resonate. They switched their recipe to be simpler in week 8 and watched churn drop to 28%. You don't need fancy analytics—just compare open rate to churn rate and adjust weekly. If open rate drops below 30%, the email isn't relevant. If churn spikes after a week, the email failed its job. Iterate fast.

One final metric: ask customers who cancel "why?" in a 2-question exit email. 60% of responses will point to the same reason (variety, storage, recipes, timing). That's your signal for next year. A greens-focused farm got 47 cancellations and received feedback: "too much spinach" (34 responses), "didn't know how to use it" (8 responses), "timing didn't work" (5 responses). They restructured their crops for year 2, added spinach-focused emails weeks 6-12, and dropped cancellations to 22. Exit data is your roadmap.
